You are mixing up benchrest precision with basic hit probability.

A 1~2 MOA rifle means the gun can group that tight under ideal conditions, not that every shot magically disappears outside that circle.

At 135 meters a human torso is far larger than a 3 inch group, it is closer to 18~20 inches wide. Even mediocre shooters hit torso targets at that distance all the time with ordinary rifles.

So yes, eight shots from a semi-auto rifle at 135 meters toward a crowd-sized target and basically accomplishing nothing just means the guy was a terrible shot. Stop pretending that distance is some impossible sniper challenge. It isn’t.

comment:
>>530850498
>energy independent since 2019

The absolute state of this meme. The US became a net energy exporter in 2019 only when you lump together petroleum products, natural gas, coal, and renewables into one big BTU bucket. Strip that away and look at actual crude oil: the US was STILL a net importer of crude at 2.2 million barrels per day in 2025. The US imported 6.5 million barrels of crude per day in 2024. Every single day. From 90 countries. "Energy independent" btw.
Why? Because over 70% of US refineries are configured for heavy crude, mostly from Canada and Mexico, not the lighter shale oil produced domestically. You can't just swap one for the other. ExxonMobil spent $2 billion over four years retooling ONE refinery to process Permian shale. Your "independence" is an accounting trick where you export light sweet crude and NatGas you can't efficiently use and import the heavy sour crude your refineries actually need.

>we spent 47 years building our oil infrastructure
Yeah, about that treadmill you built. Production from wells that came online in 2023 or earlier dropped by 4.3 million barrels per day within a single year. The industry had to drill over 15,000 new wells in 2024 just to offset that and eke out a tiny net gain. The average shale well loses 74% of its production in the first year. The IEA says if all investment in tight oil stopped, production would crash by over 35% within 12 months. Compare that to conventional Middle Eastern supergiant fields that decline slowly over decades. Your "infrastructure" is a Red Queen's race where you sprint to stay in place. Shale crude production actually peaked in November 2023 and has been sliding roughly 2% since then. 60% of Permian Tier-1 sweet spots are already drilled, with an estimated 3.7 years of premium inventory remaining at current rates. But sure, "built like fuck" lmao.

There was a draft pool of 27 million men during Vietnam. Of the 27m, only 2.2 million were actually sent a notice. Of that 2.2m, only 648,000 men were actually successfully drafted. The rest dodged. Of the dodgers, the federal government won't admit exactly how many men were caught and convicted, but it is some number between 3,000 and 8,000. comment:
William Z. Ripley, in his seminal 1899 work The Races of Europe, provided one of the most influential (and now largely suppressed) taxonomic classifications of the European continent. Writing at a time when anthropology was still an empirical science rather than a tool for political agitation, Ripley identified three primary “racial types” (what we would call sub-races or phenotypes) that constitute the European population.

The Teutonic (Nordic) Type
Physical Characteristics: Characterized by long heads (dolichocephalic), tall stature, narrow noses, and a fair complexion—blonde hair and blue or grey eyes.
Distribution: Concentrated primarily in Northern Europe, specifically around the Baltic and North Seas (Scandinavia, Northern Germany, the Netherlands, and Britain).

The Alpine Type
Physical Characteristics: Defined by broad, round heads (brachycephalic), shorter, stockier stature, and intermediate pigmentation (often brown hair and hazel or light-brown eyes).
Distribution: Centered in the mountainous heart of Europe—the Alpine regions of France, Switzerland, Southern Germany, and into parts of Central and Eastern Europe.

The Mediterranean Type
Physical Characteristics: Possessing long heads (dolichocephalic) similar to the Teutonic type but with much darker pigmentation—dark hair, dark eyes, and olive or swarthy skin. Stature is generally shorter than the Teutonic type.
Distribution: Found throughout Southern Europe, the Mediterranean basin, and the coastlines of Western Europe.

Ripley’s work was not merely about biology; it was about the correlation between race and social environment. He observed that these types did not merely exist in a vacuum but had distinct geographic “homes” that shaped their culture, social structure, and temperament.
For those interested in the preservation of European identity, Ripley’s work is essential because it illustrates the reality of European diversity—it is not a monolith. He documented the specific genetic and physical heritages that define the various European nations.

Today, his work is attacked by the academic left because it acknowledges the biological reality of racial sub-types and the idea that these types are not interchangeable. The modern “blank slate” orthodoxy hates Ripley because he provided a scientific framework to talk about blood, heritage, and the biological foundations of nations. In the current climate, simply citing him is considered “controversial,” which is the best proof that his observations about the specific, immutable nature of these groups remain dangerously—for the globalists—accurate.

No, you can quit, the problem is that most people who take it are fatties and once they quit then they regain all their fat because they lack self control. Because of that, most people stay on it.

Taking it for a couple months to lean up is what I’m doing though. About to enter week five on Tuesday.

The health risks are mostly fear mongering, especially the blindness. It’s some kind of ocular nerve stroke that you have something like a .0004% chance of having naturally and people on ozempic have their odds go up to something like .0007%. However, these strokes occur in people who have diabetes at an increased rate and because lots of people take ozempic for diabetes, there’s nothing that indicates the ocular stroke is caused by the ozempic. So there’s nothing definitive that ozempic causes an increase in spontaneous blindness.

The functional cause of this ocular stroke is high blood pressure, so if you don’t have issues with that then you should be good. I take some supplements that help mitigate high blood pressure just in case, as well as general vitamins to ensure I’m getting the necessary nutrients since I’m eating less.
